author: David Reed
summary: Closes PRODUCTION_READINESS B6. chg-11: decision_id was in the agents' forced tool schema, so the model minted a unique-constrained key; it is now server-side and absent from the schema (new DecisionDraft response model). chg-12: the decision INSERT rolls back on IntegrityError so the real error propagates instead of a masking PendingRollbackError. Verified live: the same case submitted twice now returns 200 twice with distinct ids; the second previously 500'd.

changes.description: Introduce DecisionDraft as the response_model for both agent tiers (status, confidence_score, rationale, cited_evidence_ids only) and mint decision_id server-side via AuthorizationDecision's default_factory. The model is no longer asked for an identifier, so it cannot supply one. Explicit ids are still honoured for the deterministic escape hatches (PREESC-, SCOPE-).
changes.failure_pattern: Resubmitting an already-decided clinical case returned HTTP 500. Observed 2026-07-22 while capturing a README screenshot: the first submission of the provider demo case succeeded and every subsequent one failed with 'UNIQUE constraint failed: authorization_decisions.decision_id'.
changes.root_cause: DecisionAgent.run() and MedicalDirectorAgent.run() passed response_model=AuthorizationDecision to BaseAgent.execute(), which builds the forced tool schema from response_model.model_json_schema(). decision_id is a field on AuthorizationDecision, so it was in the tool schema and the model populated it. That value was written to authorization_decisions.decision_id, declared String(50), unique=True, index=True. The model is deterministic for identical input, so the same case reproduced the same id. No code in src/ ever constructed a decision id for the normal path — grepping the observed prefixes (PA-, dec_) returned no matches in the codebase.

evidence.note: sqlite3.IntegrityError: UNIQUE constraint failed: authorization_decisions.decision_id, parameters ('dec_p_demo_72148', 'demo_1784780627924', 'AUTO_APPROVED', 0.98, ...). request_id was unique per submission; only the model-supplied decision_id collided.

Three runs produced three different id shapes (PA-SYN-2048-001, PA-SYN-3117-72148, dec_p_demo_72148), confirming the format was model-chosen. Grepping PA-/dec_ across src/ returns no matches: nothing in the codebase ever built one.

Pre-fix, 5 of 6 fail: decision_id present in the tool schema; two runs of the same case both return 'dec_p_demo_72148'. Post-fix all 6 pass. Live verification: identical case submitted twice returned PA-6f8905bf9b8a4f18 and PA-038e4eb1c2334b92, both AUTO_APPROVED at 0.96, two rows persisted, zero 500s.

predicted_fixes: Submitting the identical clinical case twice returns 200 both times with two distinct decision_id values and two persisted rows.
decision_id is absent from the tool schema handed to Claude, so no model output can influence it.
A model that emits decision_id anyway has that value discarded rather than persisted.
Pre-flight (PREESC-) and scope-violation (SCOPE-) decisions keep their explicit deterministic ids.
No change to clinical outcomes: status, confidence and rationale are still entirely model-produced, so the golden-set accuracy gate is unaffected.
risk_cases: GC-018 / GC-019 (anti-hallucination): the tool schema changed shape, so a malformed structured output would surface here first.
Any test or caller constructing AuthorizationDecision positionally, or asserting on a specific decision_id format.
The P-5 evidence-grounding detector reads cited_evidence_ids, which now round-trips through DecisionDraft — a dropped field would silently disable grounding.
changes.why_this_component: The defect is in the agent's output contract, not its reasoning. Narrowing the response_model is the smallest surface that makes the failure impossible: an identifier the model is never asked for cannot be supplied, colliding or otherwise. Changing the prompt would have been advisory; changing the schema is structural. No prompt references decision_id, so PROMPT_REGISTRY stays at v2.7.
changes.rollback_plan: git revert the chg-11 commit. Restoring response_model=AuthorizationDecision returns the model-supplied id; the default_factory on decision_id is harmless if left in place (an explicit model-supplied value overrides it). No migration, no data change. Rows already written with PA-<uuid> ids remain valid — the column has no format constraint.

changes.description: Roll the async session back when the decision INSERT raises IntegrityError, log authorization_decision_write_failed, and re-raise. Not error recovery — the write genuinely failed — but it stops a poisoned session turning the next statement into a PendingRollbackError that hides the real cause.
changes.failure_pattern: The B6 traceback surfaced 'PendingRollbackError: This Session's transaction has been rolled back due to a previous exception during flush' rather than the IntegrityError that actually caused it. An operator reading the log learns the session is broken, not why.
changes.root_cause: DecisionRepository.create() called session.flush() without handling failure. After the IntegrityError the session stays in a rolled-back-pending state, so the caller's next statement — the audit write in the submit route — raises PendingRollbackError and masks the original exception.

evidence.note: PendingRollbackError raised from the audit write masked the IntegrityError, which appeared only in the '(Background on this error...)' tail.
Pre-fix, the rollback assertion fails ('Expected rollback to have been awaited once. Awaited 0 times'). Post-fix all 3 pass, including that the success path never rolls back.

predicted_fixes: An IntegrityError on the decision write propagates as an IntegrityError, not a PendingRollbackError.
The session is rolled back exactly once on the failure path and never on the success path.
risk_cases: Rolling back inside the repository discards any uncommitted work the caller had staged in the same transaction — acceptable here because the submit route's audit records are flushed pre-write by design, but it would matter if a future caller batched writes.
changes.why_this_component: The masking happens at the point of failure, so the handler belongs there. Putting it in the route would leave every other caller of DecisionRepository.create() exposed to the same masking.
changes.rollback_plan: git revert the chg-12 commit. Removing the try/except restores the bare flush; behaviour returns to masking-on-failure. No migration, no data change.
